text.title: Security as Code (Policy as Code) Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules grounding the Security as Code (Policy as Code) methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: PRs MUST be blocked on critical-severity CVEs in dependencies (SCA) and container images. Block threshold is severity >= 7.0 (CVSS v3) with a known fix.
rule.rationale: Catching vulns at PR-time costs minutes; catching at runtime costs hours-to-days. Threshold avoids alert fatigue from low-severity findings.

rule.statement: Admission-controller policies (Kyverno / OPA Gatekeeper) MUST run in enforce mode for new namespaces by default. Exception requires a documented waiver with an expiry date.
rule.rationale: Audit-only policies provide false comfort; teams ignore violations they never see. Waivers with expiry force re-evaluation.

rule.statement: All production images MUST be signed at build (cosign) and verified at admission (cosign verify or Sigstore policy controller). Unsigned images are rejected.
rule.rationale: Image signing closes the supply-chain gap between build and run; signature verification prevents typosquat / hijacked-registry attacks.

rule.statement: Every PR runs SAST (Semgrep / CodeQL) + secrets scan (gitleaks / trufflehog). Findings of severity >= high block the PR.
rule.rationale: SAST + secret scans catch low-hanging fruit (SQL injection, hardcoded keys) before they reach review.

rule.statement: Admission-controller and runtime-policy enforcement MUST emit structured audit events to a SIEM or central log store. Logs include policy id, namespace, principal, decision, and resource.
rule.rationale: Without audit, policy violations are invisible; the same controls that block bad changes also surface attack attempts.

rule.statement: Policies (Kyverno, OPA, Rego) MUST live in version control alongside infrastructure code. Changes go via PR with review; no console-level policy edits.
rule.rationale: Policy drift is identical to config drift; PRs + review surface intent and history.
